<findings>

[observation] [info] Cohesion of 0.5 and coupling of 0.5 are well-balanced for a small self-contained module; the zone is appropriately sized and should remain stable unless crash-recovery concerns expand significantly.
[observation] [info] Including use-crash-recovery.test.ts alongside crash-detector.test.ts indicates both the pure service and its React hook are tested — good coverage breadth for a resilience-critical feature.
[observation] [info] The crash zone has a bidirectional import relationship with web-viewer (3 outbound, 2 inbound); verify that the inbound imports are from the crash/index.ts barrel only and not directly into internal crash files, which would erode encapsulation.
[pattern] [warning] No policy test currently verifies that web-viewer's 2 inbound imports to crash target only crash/index.ts — direct imports into crash/crash-detector.ts or crash internals would erode encapsulation without failing any existing test
[relationship] [warning] crash ↔ web-viewer (3+2 crossings) and panel ↔ web-viewer (11+4 crossings) both create bidirectional satellite relationships with the same hub zone; the pattern of multiple low-cohesion satellite zones bidirectionally coupled to one high-cohesion core zone is a hub-and-spoke anti-pattern that makes web-viewer changes high-risk.
[relationship] [info] viewer-crash-recovery's bidirectional relationship with web-viewer (3:2 ratio) is architecturally healthier than hench-agent-monitor's (11:4 ratio) — the crash zone demonstrates that viewer sub-zones can maintain near-symmetric coupling when they expose a deliberate barrel interface
[anti-pattern] [info] crash-detector-test-support.ts is placed in tests/helpers/ implying shared utility status but is imported by exactly one test file — single-consumer helpers in a shared directory create false discoverability expectations and should either be co-located with their sole consumer or promoted to true shared status with a second consumer
[anti-pattern] [warning] crash-detector-test-support.ts re-declares constants (storage keys, loop thresholds) that exist in production crash-detector.ts rather than importing and re-exporting them — this creates a synchronization gap where production threshold changes do not automatically propagate to test assertions, making tests silently stale after constant updates
[suggestion] [warning] boundary-check.test.ts should add an assertion that crash/crash-detector.ts does not import from viewer/external.ts — the one-line import change needed has been identified but has no automated guard preventing it from being re-introduced or missed in review.

</findings>

<insights>

- The crash module is cleanly encapsulated with its own index.ts barrel, making it a well-bounded sub-system within the viewer
- Co-location of a test-support helper (crash-detector-test-support.ts) alongside the unit tests is a good pattern that prevents test-helper sprawl
- The zone imports from web-viewer (3 imports) and is consumed by web-viewer (2 imports) — a bidirectional relationship that should be monitored to avoid circular dependencies
- The crash zone has a bidirectional import relationship with web-viewer (3 outbound, 2 inbound); verify that the inbound imports are from the crash/index.ts barrel only and not directly into internal crash files, which would erode encapsulation.
- Including use-crash-recovery.test.ts alongside crash-detector.test.ts indicates both the pure service and its React hook are tested — good coverage breadth for a resilience-critical feature.
- Cohesion of 0.5 and coupling of 0.5 are well-balanced for a small self-contained module; the zone is appropriately sized and should remain stable unless crash-recovery concerns expand significantly.
- crash ↔ web-viewer (3+2 crossings) and panel ↔ web-viewer (11+4 crossings) both create bidirectional satellite relationships with the same hub zone; the pattern of multiple low-cohesion satellite zones bidirectionally coupled to one high-cohesion core zone is a hub-and-spoke anti-pattern that makes web-viewer changes high-risk.
- viewer-crash-recovery's bidirectional relationship with web-viewer (3:2 ratio) is architecturally healthier than hench-agent-monitor's (11:4 ratio) — the crash zone demonstrates that viewer sub-zones can maintain near-symmetric coupling when they expose a deliberate barrel interface
- No policy test currently verifies that web-viewer's 2 inbound imports to crash target only crash/index.ts — direct imports into crash/crash-detector.ts or crash internals would erode encapsulation without failing any existing test
- crash-detector-test-support.ts exports constants (HEARTBEAT_KEY, CRASH_LOOP_WINDOW_MS, CRASH_LOOP_THRESHOLD, etc.) that mirror internal implementation details of crash-detector.ts — if these constants are re-declared independently rather than imported from production code, any change to the production threshold values requires a parallel manual update to the test-support file, creating a silent drift risk
- crash-detector-test-support.ts lives in tests/helpers/ (a shared location) but is consumed by exactly one test file (crash-detector.test.ts) — the shared placement overstates reuse; if a second zone ever imports it, a test-zone cross-import edge would appear that violates the test-isolation invariant
- crash-detector-test-support.ts re-declares constants (storage keys, loop thresholds) that exist in production crash-detector.ts rather than importing and re-exporting them — this creates a synchronization gap where production threshold changes do not automatically propagate to test assertions, making tests silently stale after constant updates
- crash-detector-test-support.ts is placed in tests/helpers/ implying shared utility status but is imported by exactly one test file — single-consumer helpers in a shared directory create false discoverability expectations and should either be co-located with their sole consumer or promoted to true shared status with a second consumer
- crash-detector.ts currently imports ViewId via 'import type { ViewId } from "../external.js"' (line 16), routing through the viewer gateway rather than directly to the shared layer. The type-only qualifier means no runtime cycle exists today, but the structural zone cycle persists and the intended architectural isolation (crash-detector independent of viewer layer) is not achieved.
- boundary-check.test.ts should add an assertion that crash/crash-detector.ts does not import from viewer/external.ts — the one-line import change needed has been identified but has no automated guard preventing it from being re-introduced or missed in review.
- [call graph] 73 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
